Takeaways from the 91 Extreme Developing+ Course

Honestly, the 91 Extreme Developing+ course exists to fix the one development complaint everyone keeps making: "not enough time". My company pushed me into it, so I figured I'd write up the takeaways.
Yep, you read that right. I got pushed into it.
So I just had to suck it up and go. QQ
What's the course actually about? Like 91 himself says on the

It's about solving the practical "not enough time" problem everyone keeps complaining about. Because of "not enough time", I can't write unit tests, can't refactor, can't TDD, can't code-review, can't do my best work.
So 91 also has a formula for extreme developing:
Productivity = Your design skill (ability) + Optimized dev environment and config (divine tools) + The right way to develop (technique)
The ability part I'll skip — that grows with long-term practice. But the other two are way harder, and that's what this course focuses on: optimizing your dev environment and your way of developing.
Because of COVID, I took it via Zoom. Class ran 9:00 AM to 6:00 PM. Don't worry about your bladder — the instructor gives you a few minutes for bathroom breaks.
of resources before the class starts. You have to prep all of it ahead of time — software to install, dev-tool setup, things to be aware of — or you're going to have an unproductive day.
(Unfortunately, that was me. I was swamped with other stuff at the time, but 91 was really helpful and walked me through the setup. QQ)

At the start of class, 91 asks for a volunteer to demo their normal workflow. The example is Tennis (the classic TDD intro). As the volunteer codes, 91 calls out which things could be improved. You'll spot the friction points just from watching.
Then 91 takes the stage and demonstrates what
looks like (like a machine gun firing code). I really have to admit it's blazingly fast. Same Tennis example, but in 91's hands it's basically light-speed.
Don't worry about language compatibility either — 91 demos in nearly every language. I remember Java, Python, C#, JavaScript all making an appearance. Language is not a barrier.
If your main language is JavaScript, 91 will recommend WebStorm. Watching him use WebStorm even once is enough to make you go "wow" — it's genuinely powerful.

you should use the right tool (e.g., an IDE) for the job. Whenever possible, let the IDE do the heavy lifting. Say you want to extract part of a JS file into a module — in the traditional flow you'd manually create the
file, move the code over, then come back to the original file and write the
With an IDE, you can extract a module instantly, and the import gets written for you automatically. That cuts out a lot of manual file-juggling.
But just installing an IDE doesn't magically speed you up — you still have to pair it with dev environment configs. That's the "optimized dev environment and config" part of the formula.
With a well-optimized setup you can do things like type
Next is the editor used in class: Vim. A good IDE paired with Vim is genuinely next-level fast. You'll watch 91 type stuff like viw, zz, zh — and a pile of work just happens. The
is basically out of the picture.
I'll admit Vim/Vi is brutal at first. Here's a Vim cheat sheet:
With Vim/Vi you can do certain things really fast, like quickly select a word and copy-paste:
Of course, Vim/Vi will feel rough at first. I personally do most of my work in VSCode, so I installed the Vim extension there. If you're interested, check out my older post "
Get Started with Vim in 10 Minutes Using VSCode (Beginner Edition)
" (article in Mandarin) as an entry point.

One side note — if your Vim/Vi isn't smooth yet, don't drop it into your work. It'll actually slow you down. Get comfortable with a decent baseline speed first, then bring it into your daily workflow.

91 also recommends not spending too much time on it each day — 15 minutes daily is plenty. Slow growth is steadier. Practice with Tennis, and record yourself each time. You'll see your own progress by reviewing the videos.

You'll probably have question marks all over your head during the course, but take some time to digest afterward and you'll realize how much you got out of it. The point is watching how 91 maximizes his own time. What IDE he uses, whether he uses Vim — those are personal calls. The takeaway is:
think about how to use your time well, and find faster ways to develop
This got long. To help my Vim muscle memory, I'm actually typing this post in Vim too. Wrapping up here.
